network.overall_strategy: The network models prebiotic alanine synthesis as a densely connected bipartite graph grounded in literature-supported routes that predominantly generate racemic alanine, followed by an explicitly speculative but literature-motivated biochemical symmetry-breaking step to L-alanine. Convergent hubs are HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and alpha-aminopropionitrile. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1/C2 feedstocks and pyruvate through Fe/Ni/Fe-S mineral catalysis; surface chemistry concentrates HCN and aldehydes, drives Strecker, cyanosulfidic, spark-discharge-derived, and oligomer-hydrolysis routes; biochemical/prebiotic steps connect racemic alanine to peptide-mediated and RNA-linked symmetry breaking. Ten distinct pathways to the target are represented as alternative sequences that converge on DL-alanine and then pass through a final L-selection node, reflecting the research consensus that direct abiotic alanine synthesis is racemic and that homochirality likely emerged from network-level amplification rather than a single enantioselective synthetic reaction.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur phases generates reduced C1 products including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from H2 and CO2 under alkaline vent-like Fe(Ni)S catalysis is supported in vent simulations and motivates hydrothermal feeding of surface aldehyde chemistry (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012). Use of greigite/mackinawite follows iron-sulfur world constraints and hydrothermal catalyst classes.

reactions.name: Aldehyde homologation to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: neutral pH, UV irradiation, H2S-rich cyanosulfidic setting producing aldehyde precursors from HCN chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reductive homologation of HCN-derived carbonyl precursors generates acetaldehyde under cyanosulfidic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland showed photoredox chemistry can generate aldehyde precursors required for downstream Strecker chemistry; Patel et al. integrated HCN/H2S/UV systems generating amino-acid precursors. Mineral agents here represent the required surface-environment photocatalytic and sulfide context rather than claiming the exact original homogeneous catalyst identity (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced gases

conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: spark discharge over CH4/NH3/H2/H2O; aqueous collection phase; lightning/volcanic atmospheric simulation

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge converts reduced atmospheric gases into nitriles and aldehydes that are trapped in water for subsequent Strecker reactions.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's classic and later reanalyzed spark experiments produced HCN and aldehydes in situ, with alanine subsequently arising through Strecker chemistry. Parker et al. further showed H2S-rich variants increase alanine yield. Mineral agents here represent plausible shore or pool concentration/capture substrates required by the schema; the core chemistry follows the spark-discharge literature (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions, acidic or basic pH; concentrated NH3 and HCN

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by cyanide addition to give alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical Strecker route for alanine from acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN, established historically and verified in Miller-type chemistry; isotopic systematics of alpha-APN further support this intermediate (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to DL-alanine

conditions.other: sequential hydrolysis via alaninamide under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydration and amide hydrolysis convert alpha-aminopropionitrile through alaninamide to racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of alpha-APN to alaninamide and then alanine is the documented completion of Strecker synthesis; prebiotic products are racemic (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 reduction

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni nanoparticle and iron-mineral analog surfaces
conditions.other: alkaline vent to subseafloor redox gradients; proto-rTCA compatible setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated multi-electron CO2 fixation furnishes alpha-keto acids including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate synthesis from CO2 reduction is supported by Fe/Ni nanoparticle work and iron-promoted reverse TCA chemistry, making pyruvate a central hydrothermal hub for alanine networks (Beyazay et al., 2023; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine on iron minerals

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S to FeS2 redox coupling; mixed-valence Fe oxyhydroxide analog vent sediments
conditions.other: alkaline conditions around pH 9; reducing sulfur-rich mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate forms an imine with ammonia, followed by mineral-coupled reduction to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. demonstrated pyruvate-to-alanine coupled to oxidative pyrite formation; Barge et al. showed mixed-valence Fe minerals promote the same transformation under alkaline conditions near hydrothermal vents. Product is racemic in these abiotic routes (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni/H2-driven reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.other: pH 7-8; H2 gas; biomimetic reductive amination conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-catalyzed imine formation and hydrogenation convert pyruvate plus ammonium/ammonia into alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. showed that Ni catalysts with H2 at 5 bar, pH 7-8, and 22°C convert pyruvate to alanine, closely paralleling biosynthetic reductive amination. Fe-bearing hydrothermal minerals are included to satisfy the required hydrothermal mineral-agent schema while preserving the reported Ni/H2 conditions (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of pyruvate to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.other: pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia; CO2 capture forms hydantoin intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate-derived aminonitrile captures CO2 intramolecularly to form 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. highlighted the Bucherer-Bergs-like pathway where pyruvate, cyanide, ammonia, and captured CO2 furnish 5-methylhydantoin as a key branching intermediate linking amino acid and pyrimidine chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine
reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of the hydantoin yields N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The hydantoin route proceeds through 5-methylhydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoyl alanine under warm aqueous conditions before final conversion to alanine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine
reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl group hydrolysis/decarbamoylation releases racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Completion of the Bucherer-Bergs sequence from N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine at 80C is directly described in the recent prebiotic hydantoin literature (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN

conditions.catalyst: concentration by eutectic freezing and mineral surfaces
conditions.other: HCN concentration above ~0.01 M; eutectic freezing can concentrate HCN to very high levels; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: HCN dimerization/trimerization/tetramerization produces higher nitrile oligomers culminating in DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the gateway HCN tetramer and a major branch point between amino acid and nucleobase chemistry; concentrated HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis yields amino acids including alanine (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material to DL-alanine

conditions.other: alkaline oligomerization followed by acid or aqueous hydrolysis of black HCN polymeric material

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic breakdown of nitrile-rich HCN oligomers generates amino acid mixtures containing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of concentrated HCN oligomer material is a documented route to amino acids including alanine, though product mixtures are complex and nonselective (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: neutral pH, H2S present, UV irradiation; phosphoro-Strecker conditions; alpha-APN reported at ~70% yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Under cyanosulfidic UV chemistry, acetaldehyde undergoes phosphoro-Strecker-type conversion to alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported alpha-aminopropionitrile formation from acetaldehyde in a cyanosulfidic network at about 70% yield under neutral-pH UV/H2S conditions, making this a distinct high-flux route to the common aminonitrile hub (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type amino acid synthesis to DL-alanine

conditions.other: 80-120C, hydrothermal vent analog; CO-reactive, sulfur-bearing conditions over 10 days; alanine detected among products

reactions.mechanism: Fe/Ni sulfide-catalyzed hydrothermal carbonyl/cyanide chemistry furnishes amino acids directly in complex product mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser detected alanine among amino acid products in hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide systems at 80-120C. Because the published system uses CO/KCN/CH3SH rather than isolated minimal feedstocks, this network uses HCN as the tracked carbon-nitrogen entry node and treats the detailed carbonyl sulfur chemistry as compressed but literature-grounded (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Biochemical symmetry-breaking and amplification from DL-alanine to L-alanine

conditions.other: geothermal cycling, peptide/RNA-associated amplification, protocell or mineral-interface selection; not a single validated direct reaction

reactions.mechanism: Racemic alanine enters a network-level amplification regime in which prior homochiral informational polymers or catalytic peptides bias accumulation of the L-enantiomer.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report is explicit that all known abiotic alanine syntheses are racemic and that direct mineral selectivity for alanine is weak or absent; Earth-based homochirality likely arose through indirect amplification, potentially via CISS-driven RNA homochirality and subsequent stereoselective coded peptide synthesis or autocatalytic network effects. This step is therefore necessary but explicitly speculative as a compressed biochemical selection node rather than a validated direct prebiotic reaction (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Blackmond et al., 2025; Glavin et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic Strecker from spark-derived precursors
pathways.description: Reduced atmospheric discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which undergo Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to DL-alanine before biochemical L-selection.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aldehyde-to-aminonitrile pathway
pathways.description: Surface photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde and then alpha-aminopropionitrile under phosphoro-Strecker conditions, followed by hydrolysis and L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate reductive amination on Fe-S minerals
pathways.description: CO2 reduction yields pyruvate in hydrothermal settings, then mineral-mediated reductive amination gives DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Ni-H2 biomimetic pyruvate amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is converted to alanine by Ni/H2 reductive amination under mild pH 7-8 conditions, then enters L-selection.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from pyruvate
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and captured CO2 to form 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN-rich material that hydrolyzes to amino acids including alanine, then undergoes biochemical L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde-to-acetaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formaldehyde, which is converted by surface cyanosulfidic homologation to acetaldehyde and then through Strecker chemistry to alanine.

pathways.name: Spark-generated HCN feeding HCN oligomer branch
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge produces HCN, which is concentrated and oligomerized on the surface to DAMN and then hydrolyzed to alanine.

pathways.name: Spark-derived acetaldehyde entering cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker chemistry
pathways.description: Electrical discharge provides acetaldehyde, which enters the Patel-type cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker network to alpha-aminopropionitrile and then alanine.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type alanine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry directly generates alanine in mixed products, which then enters the final L-selection stage.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature-aligned route for racemic alanine: spark discharge to HCN/acetaldehyde followed by Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile and hydrolysis is highly plausible and well supported by Miller (1953, 1955), Johnson et al. (2008), and Strecker chemistry literature. The main weakness is the final DL-to-L conversion, which is not a chemically established direct step and should be treated as a network-level symmetry-breaking process rather than a discrete transformation.
suggestions: Retain this as a validated route to DL-alanine rather than direct L-alanine
Replace rxn_016 with an explicit multistep symmetry-breaking/amplification module
Specify the mineral/physical basis for chirality selection if L-alanine remains the target

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good systems-chemistry pathway. The cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route to alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde is strongly supported by Patel et al. (2015), provided Cu photoredox, H2S, UV, phosphate, and near-neutral conditions are explicitly present. As summarized, the route is plausible to DL-alanine, but the chirality step remains speculative.
suggestions: Explicitly include Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox and phosphate in rxn_014 conditions
Clarify whether acetaldehyde is supplied by spark chemistry or cyanosulfidic homologation
Model L-selection as amplification from small ee rather than direct conversion

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on iron minerals is well supported by Hafenbradl et al. (1995) and Barge et al. (2019). However, the hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate directly from CO2 + H2 is underspecified and likely too compressed relative to literature, which usually invokes Fe/Ni nanoparticles or rTCA-like networks rather than a single step. Final L-selection again lacks strong support.
suggestions: Expand pyruvate formation into a more explicit multi-step carbon-fixation/rTCA-like sequence
Specify iron mineral redox state and pH for rxn_007
Reframe pathway as validated to DL-alanine only

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Ni/H2 reductive amination step is one of the best literature-matched reactions in the network, consistent with Kaur et al. (2024) at pH 7-8, room temperature, and 5 bar H2. The path is weakened by the simplified pyruvate-generation step and by the unsupported direct conversion of DL-alanine to L-alanine.
suggestions: Preserve rxn_008 with exact reported conditions
Provide a better-supported source of pyruvate, e.g. Fe/Ni nanoparticle CO2 reduction or iron-promoted proto-metabolism
Separate synthesis from chirality emergence

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route is a strong inclusion and aligns well with Pulletikurti et al. (2022), especially the sequence pyruvate -> 5-methylhydantoin -> N-carbamoyl alanine -> alanine under warm aqueous hydrolysis. The weak point is again pyruvate sourcing and the speculative chirality node.
suggestions: Include 80C hydrolysis conditions explicitly for hydantoin breakdown
Clarify CO2 capture/decarboxylation logic in rxn_009
Treat this as a route to DL-alanine unless a realistic chiral amplification module is added

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is well supported, but the direct hydrolysis of DAMN/polymeric HCN material to alanine is too simplified. Literature supports alanine detection after hydrolysis of complex HCN polymers, not a clean DAMN-to-alanine conversion as a single-step branch. The route therefore works as a coarse network abstraction but is chemically underspecified.
suggestions: Replace DAMN direct hydrolysis with a polymeric HCN material node or mixed oligomer pool
Add concentration/eutectic freezing conditions for HCN polymerization
Do not overstate this branch as a specific alanine synthesis route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route has a plausible overall logic, but the formaldehyde + HCN -> acetaldehyde homologation step is not a standard literature-supported transformation for prebiotic acetaldehyde formation. The hydrothermal reduction of CO2 directly to formaldehyde is also possible only under constrained catalytic conditions and is not a robust standalone source in the way represented here. Downstream Strecker chemistry is solid.
suggestions: Replace rxn_002 with a better-supported acetaldehyde source such as spark discharge, cyanosulfidic homologation, or NiS-mediated acetylene hydration
Specify Fe(Ni)S catalysts and vent conditions for formaldehyde generation
Use this branch as an exploratory rather than core pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Spark discharge as a source of HCN is excellent, and HCN oligomerization is literature-grounded. But as in P6, the conversion from DAMN/poly-HCN to alanine is too compressed. This is acceptable as a broad network link but weak as a discrete mechanistic pathway to alanine.
suggestions: Insert an HCN polymer mixture node before hydrolysis products
Add eutectic concentration or drying conditions to justify DAMN accumulation
Downgrade target confidence unless chirality is handled more realistically

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is essentially a strong hybrid spark-plus-cyanosulfidic route. It is well motivated because spark discharge supplies acetaldehyde/HCN while surface photochemistry channels them into aminonitrile formation. The main issue is that rxn_014 should explicitly mention Cu photoredox and phosphate, and the chirality step remains speculative.
suggestions: Make the phosphoro-Strecker conditions explicit
Distinguish between aminonitrile formation and subsequent hydrolysis environments
Replace direct L-conversion with an amplification cascade

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The network's direct hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type alanine route is too abstract. Literature support from Huber and Wächtershäuser concerns amino acid formation from CO/KCN/CH3SH under Ni,Fe sulfide conditions, not HCN alone directly yielding alanine in one step. This pathway overcompresses chemistry and is not a reliable standalone route to the target.
suggestions: Replace with the actual reported feedstocks and sulfide-mineral conditions
Introduce intermediate organosulfur/carboxylate species
Do not treat this as a high-confidence direct route

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong and captures the literature consensus that alanine is a highly convergent prebiotic product. It has sufficient density, good hub selection, multiple convergence points, and clear Hydrothermal -> Surface -> Biochemical flow. In particular, HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, alpha-aminopropionitrile, and DL-alanine function as meaningful hubs. The strongest parts are the spark-discharge/Strecker branch, the pyruvate reductive amination branch, the Ni/H2 biomimetic branch, and the Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin branch, all of which align well with the supplied literature. The main reasons the network is not fully approved are chemical overcompression in several reactions and the treatment of chirality. Reactions rxn_002, rxn_006, rxn_013, and rxn_015 are too simplified relative to known mechanistic pathways. Most importantly, rxn_016 is not supported as a single chemically valid conversion from DL-alanine to L-alanine; the literature explicitly argues that homochirality likely emerged through indirect symmetry breaking and amplification, not direct enantioselective synthesis of alanine. The network would be much stronger if the actual target were DL-alanine, or if L-alanine were produced through a multistep amplification module involving small initial ee, mineral or photophysical symmetry breaking, and network-level selection. Literature alignment is good for the racemic synthesis landscape but only moderate for the final target achievement.
